title: File uploads corrupted for larger files (PDFs, possibly other types) since ~March 31 deployment
problem: Files uploaded via the task attachment feature are silently corrupted. Downloaded files are non-empty but unreadable/damaged. The issue is size-dependent: smaller files (e.g., simple PDFs, most images) upload correctly, but larger business documents (multi-page reports, contracts — likely several MB+) are consistently corrupted. At least one spreadsheet was also affected, suggesting this is not PDF-specific but a general large-file upload problem.
root_cause_hypothesis: A deployment around Tuesday–Wednesday of last week (~March 31–April 1) likely introduced a regression in the file upload pipeline. Probable causes: (1) a change to multipart upload chunking that truncates or misassembles chunks for files exceeding a threshold, (2) a misconfigured request body size limit (e.g., nginx/proxy layer) that silently truncates payloads, or (3) an encoding/content-type handling change that corrupts binary data in larger payloads where chunked transfer is triggered.
reproduction_steps: Select a PDF file that is several megabytes (e.g., a 5+ page report or contract)
Attempt to open it — the PDF viewer will report the file as damaged/corrupted
Repeat with a small/simple PDF (1 page, < 1 MB) to confirm it uploads correctly
environment: Affects multiple customers across unknown browsers/OS. No specific environment dependency reported — appears to be a server-side issue. The attach-button upload flow is the confirmed vector.
impact: Multiple customers are affected. The file attachment feature is heavily used for business-critical documents (contracts, reports). Teams have been unable to reliably share key documents for approximately one week, forcing workarounds or blocking workflows entirely.
recommended_fix: 1. Check deployment logs for changes shipped Tuesday March 31 – Wednesday April 1. Focus on file upload handling, storage layer, proxy/gateway config, and any dependency updates. 2. Compare uploaded file bytes vs. original file bytes for a large test PDF to identify where corruption occurs (truncation, encoding, chunk reassembly). 3. Check for size-related thresholds in the upload path (proxy body limits, chunk size configs, streaming vs. buffered handling). 4. Test uploads at varying file sizes to identify the exact breakpoint.
proposed_test_case: Upload files of increasing sizes (500 KB, 1 MB, 2 MB, 5 MB, 10 MB, 20 MB) across multiple file types (PDF, XLSX, PNG). Download each and verify byte-for-byte integrity against the original using checksums. The test should assert that the downloaded file's hash matches the uploaded file's hash for all sizes and types.
information_gaps: Exact file size threshold where corruption begins (developer can determine via systematic testing)
Whether the corruption is truncation, byte mangling, or encoding-related (developer can compare uploaded vs. downloaded bytes)
Exact deployment or commit that introduced the regression (check deployment logs for March 31–April 2)
Browser and OS details of affected users (unlikely to matter given server-side hypothesis, but not confirmed)
